nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef flats, lagoons, and reef slopes; commonly encrusts hard substrate, dead coral rubble, and rock surfaces in moderate-flow zones

decorPreferences: placement away from adjacent corals to manage spreading
open substrate areas where colony spread can be monitored

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae; target feeding is not generally required. May benefit from the presence of dissolved organics or very occasional additions of phytoplankton or amino acids. Unlike some filter feeders, Anthelia relies heavily on photosynthesis rather than active particle capture.

goodWith: other soft corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: adjacent corals without buffer zones — Anthelia encrusts rapidly and can smother slower neighbors
torch corals and hammer corals — their sweeper tentacles can sting Anthelia

summary: Anthelia is an encrusting soft coral (family Xeniidae) with distinctive 8-tentacled feathered polyps that wave in flow but do NOT pulse — distinguishing it from the closely related Xenia genus. A beginner-friendly, fast-spreading reef coral.
careNotes:
Anthelia vs. Xenia distinction: Anthelia is commonly confused with Xenia due to both belonging to family Xeniidae and sharing the 8-pinnate (feathered) tentacle structure common to all octocorals. The key morphological and behavioral difference is that Anthelia polyps do NOT exhibit the rhythmic pumping/pulsing motion that defines Xenia and its relatives (Heteroxenia, Sansibia). Anthelia polyps simply sway in ambient current. Each polyp bears exactly 8 slender, plume-like (pinnate) tentacles, identical in count to Xenia but typically described as larger and with longer pinnules. Anthelia also tends to have taller, more tubular polyp stalks (documented at 11–25 cm in A. glauca in the wild) growing from a creeping basal encrusting mat.

Growth form: Anthelia spreads by extending a flat encrusting mat (stolon) across rock and substrate surfaces, from which new polyps emerge. This growth habit is notably aggressive; colonies have been observed overtaking large sections of rockwork within weeks to months under ideal conditions. Placing new frags on isolated rubble islands or removable frag plugs is generally recommended so the colony can be pruned back or repositioned without fragging into established rockwork.

Lighting: Generally considered to do well under low-to-moderate light (approximately 50–150 PAR). Bulk Reef Supply's product listing references 150–250 PAR for their ECC strain, suggesting some acclimated cultured variants have been conditioned to higher intensities. New acquisitions are generally best started in lower-light positions and moved up gradually.

Flow: Moderate, non-laminar flow is generally preferred — enough to keep polyps gently waving and free of detritus, but not so strong as to stress the colony. Sources consistently describe moderate as the target, though some note that moderate-to-high is acceptable.

Water chemistry: Relatively tolerant compared to SPS corals. The parameters below reflect an average across five sources. Alkalinity sourced from AquariumDomain and TankFacts (8–12 dKH) averaged against BRS (8–9.5 dKH), yielding 8–11 dKH. Calcium and magnesium from BRS (400–450 ppm Ca; 1300–1450 ppm Mg) are the only source to provide these values specifically for Anthelia.

Temperature cross-source note: Sources ranged from 22–28°C across five entries. Min ~22–24°C average; max ~26–28°C. Conservative average of 23–27°C adopted. Salinity min ranged 1.023–1.025; max 1.025–1.026 — 1.024–1.026 adopted as averaged range.

Alkalinity range note: AquariumDomain and TankFacts gave 8–12 dKH; BRS gave 8–9.5 dKH. Upper bound averaged to 11 dKH.

Colony size note: A. glauca polyp stalks are documented at 11–25 cm in height in the wild per Wikipedia. In aquarium settings, the overall encrusting colony spread is the more relevant measure; reported as capable of spreading across large sections of rockwork. The adultSizeCm range of 5–30 cm reflects colony patch diameter under typical aquarium conditions.

Reef safety: Generally considered reef-safe in that Anthelia does not actively sting other corals. However, its aggressive encrusting spread is considered the primary compatibility concern — it can smother slower-growing neighbors by physically overgrowth rather than chemical means.

Broadcast spawning: A. glauca has been documented broadcasting at full moons during summer months, with larvae briefly brooded in a pharyngeal pouch. Colonies are dioecious (separate male and female).
breedingNotes: Propagation in captivity is typically achieved by placing a new rock or rubble piece adjacent to an established colony and allowing it to encrust naturally onto the new substrate over days to weeks. Direct fragging (cutting polyp stalks) is possible but colonies are said to recover more readily via natural runner extension onto new substrate than from cut frags. A. glauca has been documented reproducing sexually via broadcast spawning on natural reefs timed to full moons in summer, with larvae briefly brooded internally before release — captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: Bulk Reef Supply – ECC Waving Hand Anthelia
primary.notes: Primary source used in place of LiveAquaria (which returned 404 for all Anthelia URL attempts) and WWC (which also returned 404). BRS is a listed secondary for coral-saltwater slice and was the most complete single source for water chemistry parameters (temperature 75–78°F, salinity 1.025–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–9.5 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1300–1450 ppm, PAR 150–250).

additional.name: Whitlyn Aquatics – Anthelia Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-verified temperature (74–80°F / 23–27°C), salinity (1.024–1.026), pH (8.1–8.4), moderate flow, beginner care level, photosynthetic diet, peaceful temperament. Confirmed Anthelia does not pulse unlike Xenia. Did not provide alkalinity, calcium, or magnesium values.

additional.name: AquariumDomain – Anthelia Waving Hand Coral Species Profile
additional.notes: Cross-verified temperature (72–78°F / 22–26°C), salinity (1.023–1.025), pH (8.1–8.4), alkalinity (8–12 dKH), moderate-to-strong flow, lower placement, Indo-Pacific range. Confirmed 8 pinnate tentacles per polyp and non-pulsing behavior. Minor disagreement on flow (moderate-to-strong vs moderate); also listed care level as moderate vs beginner majority.

additional.name: Reeflings – Anthelia glauca Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-verified temperature (24–28°C / 75–82°F), salinity (1.023–1.026), pH (8.0–8.4), PAR 70–150, moderate flow, bottom placement, fast growth, peaceful temperament. Did not provide alkalinity, calcium, or magnesium values.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Anthelia glauca
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family: Xeniidae, Order: Malacalcyonacea), native range (Indo-Pacific, Red Sea to Hawaii, 8–22 m depth), morphology (8 plume-like tentacles, polyps 11–25 cm tall, non-retractable, non-pulsing), and reproductive biology (broadcast spawning, dioecious, pharyngeal brooding). Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Anthelia (coral genus taxonomy)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy confirmation (Family: Xeniidae, Order: Malacalcyonacea, Class: Octocorallia) and species list (13 known species including A. glauca, A. elongata, A. japonica, A. strumosa). Not used for husbandry.
